NetSuite's biggest drawback is the complexity and cost of implementation and customization. Out of the box it requires significant configuration to fit your specific business needs, and that almost always means relying on either internal technical resources or expensive outside consultants to get it set up properly. The user interface, while functional, feels dated compared to more modern platforms and can be unintuitive for new users, which makes onboarding and training more time consuming than it should be. Reporting is powerful but building custom reports can be cumbersome and often requires more technical knowledge than the average user has. The licensing and add-on cost structure can also be difficult to predict as the business grows, since many features that feel essential are gated behind additional modules. These friction points are manageable given the overall value NetSuite provides, but they are real enough that they add to the total cost of ownership beyond just the subscription fee.

Certain processes, like generating reports or running searches, can be slower than ideal, particularly with large datasets. These challenges don’t outweigh the benefits, but they can impact efficiency in day-to-day use.

Some of the modules seem like they were an afterthought or built in, which isn't great. The language and safe search features aren't always clear, and there are limitations in setup that just aren't obvious. This kind of hidden knowledge is frustrating. Knowing that NetSuite is primarily a general ledger would have been helpful and saved us time. The initial setup was really challenging because we didn't have any on-site IT experts, and the consultants weren't much help. We had to manually handle a lot of processes, which was tedious. Also, moving to a web store with a user-friendly interface was harder than we thought, so we ended up using Shopify instead of SuiteCommerce.

One of the challenges with NetSuite is that the user interface can sometimes feel less intuitive, especially for new users. Certain configurations and customizations may require technical expertise, which can increase dependency on developers or consultants. Additionally, troubleshooting integrations and scripts can sometimes be time-consuming, and documentation for specific use cases is not always detailed. Performance can also occasionally slow down when working with large datasets or complex saved searches.
I don't like that multi-currency settlement does not allow settlement between currencies in purchases or sales. Manufacturing planning also can't be customized depending on custom fields.

one disadvantage is that managing intricate logic (such as tolerance checks) frequently necessitates the use of scripting or saved searches as workarounds. Certain common restrictions, like line-level controls or partial billing of expenses, are not always clear-cut. Troubleshooting can be time-consuming due to unclear user interface and error messages. Some actions seem counterintuitive, such as managing system-generated records or excluding voided transactions. In general, obtaining advanced functionality may occasionally necessitate additional customization work rather than native support.

One thing I don’t like about NetSuite is that it can feel pretty complex, especially for new users. The interface isn’t always very intuitive, so it takes a while to get comfortable and really understand how everything works. Another drawback is the cost. For smaller businesses, it can be quite expensive—not only the subscription, but also the implementation and customization. It’s simply not always the most budget-friendly option. I’ve also found that some customizations and reports can be tricky to set up without technical help. At times you need external support or a specialist, which can slow progress and make changes take longer than expected. Overall, even though it’s a powerful system, there’s a clear learning curve and some added costs that come with it.

I find integrating bank and credit card feeds with NetSuite challenging. Unlike smaller software like QuickBooks, which makes this process straightforward, NetSuite requires more effort and complexity to achieve this integration.

I don’t like SDF, and I honestly don’t use it much. Early on, I found the deployment process difficult and error-prone, mainly because of missing dependencies. Since we have four SBs before we get to production, the manual deployment entry steps become especially troublesome. While I understand the benefits of workflows, I find that our company’s implementation of workflows and scripts often creates challenges when maintaining a system where the logic can live across multiple platforms. A lot of the time, simply figuring out where the issue actually resides ends up being the most time-consuming part of maintenance.
There is a lack of organization and explanation regarding invoicing.

One downside of NetSuite is that its interface can feel complex and not very intuitive for new users, so it often requires additional training to get people comfortable. Implementations and customizations can also be time-consuming and expensive, particularly when the system needs to be tailored to specific workflows. Some reporting and saved search functionality may require technical knowledge to build and maintain effectively. In addition, certain integrations with other systems can require middleware or extra development work. Finally, when you factor in licensing, implementation, and ongoing support, the overall cost can be high for smaller organizations.

The UI is slow, clunky, and outdated. I'm hopeful that NetSuite Next (next generation UI) fixes those issues. Navigating between transactions or records can be cumbersome. There also is a pretty steep learning curve around the backend language - understanding how fields and records are joined can be challenging at first. For starters, the look could feel more modern. The current UI feels like it's from the mid-2010s, and it sometimes performs like it. Waiting on fields to populate, sublists to load, transactions to save - it all just feels slow. Creating records, while similar across verticals, could be streamlined. It wasn't that easy. We got lucky in that we had a functional consultant live local to our office in northern IL, but even then we took a staged approach to implementation. Financials first, then AP and purchasing, then sales, AR, and inventory.

The least helpful using NetSuite is the 3rd party billing cannot be used with USPS. If a customer has 3rd party billing on their customer page, then USPS cannot be selected. We have to manually take the 3rd party billing off of the order to choose USPS for shipping. That is the only downside of NetSuite.

Changing the revenue recognition schedule is cumbersome. It needs to be easier to change the dates and update the future revenue recognition. Currently, you need to edit the forecast and then the plan.

I'm not a technical person and I struggle with building custom reports. I try to build custom reports on my own, but I never seem to get exactly what I need. I find the interface for this area cumbersome and confusing.

From an accounting perspective, I find that NetSuite could allow greater analysis of ledgers. Creating custom reports is very challenging, even with admin access and experience. There's a lot of trial and error involved in bringing the correct fields, and some fields aren't supported for reports. It's also challenging to pull data from different sources. The 'new' Analytics feature should address this, but it's still pretty complicated and slow. There are limitations with closed transactions in AP and AR, as there's no good capability to match transactions in general ledgers like the VAT ledger. The features available aren't good enough yet. Anything related to multi books or creating different types of reports (GAAP / Non-GAAP) isn't supported well enough. The initial setup was not easy at all.

I don't like that NetSuite doesn't use natural language to find things or reports. It's challenging because the search tools require you to know exactly how to search for something. Also, the initial setup was a bit cumbersome, though it wasn't too bad with the help of a consultant or expert.
The only thing would be when sending invoices directly from NetSuite, if there is a fail, it doesn't tell you which invoice failed to send and that can be kind of annoying.
I think data board reporting could be improved with easier drag and drop functionality. Also, item record fields often have similar names, which makes building dashboards time-consuming.
It's slow and is bad with working with data within NetSuite. It can take minutes to load a single record or even a saved search could take 20 minutes to load.
It's hard to customize certain areas without having to use custom scripts (that we can't do ourselves and have to use our Netsuite consultant to do this for us at an added cost).

The least helpful part is that when I customize a report or design an uploading file to map it to match NetSuite template, it does not have a clear guidance which item is for which actual field in the NetSuite transaction form. I need to guess and test several times. Also, when I have some questions regarding customizing reports but cannot find answer in the website, I did not know where quickly connect to the customer support to resolve my problems.

NetSuite is a powerful platform, but it’s also quite complex and costly to implement and maintain. It requires significant training, comes with a steep learning curve. In my experience, it isn’t feasible to use for project-related work in the manufacturing sector.

Sometimes, if there's a feature that doesn't work with NetSuite, it can take ages for them to address it with an enhancement because not a lot of customers may want it. Sometimes, there's no workaround, and these enhancement requests can stay in the NetSuite world for ages.

The base license covers very little, and many essential capabilities require additional paid modules or third party tools. Basic financial workflows lack automation, integrations are difficult, and reporting is limited. Many processes remain manual unless significant development resources or costly customization are added.

When downloading the Excel files from NetSuite, it's challenging to always have to change the file type to a usable format. This is an extra step for all reports that I have to download from NetSuite.

Sometimes when we try to pull the types of reports that we need, it takes a very long time because we have to go line item by line item and pull the data, so it can take hours to run. So that's very frustrating. We also don't have our scripts set up very well right now and have to do a lot of manual data manipulation and then re-upload it into NetSuite.

There hasn’t been much for me to dislike about NetSuite overall. That said, I do think the standard reporting could be a bit more robust. At times, I also wish the report layouts were easier to define and could be set up in a more polished, consistent way.

My main frustration with NetSuite is that while it is highly flexible, it often takes significant setup, customization, and technical knowledge to make processes work efficiently. Some native reports and search limitations mean teams still need workarounds or custom development to get exactly what they need. The UI in certain areas also feels dated, and performance can slow down when using saved searches, scripts, or large datasets. Overall, it is powerful, but not always as user-friendly or efficient out of the box as it could be.

The basic interface is kind of clunky. I don't like that when entering transactions, the decimal amounts aren't automatically in the correct spots based on what seems like an improper system assumption. It seems like a setting glitch that I haven't been able to fix. It would be better if users could customize how they want to input numbers and the format it shows up in.

The implementation is a long process, but very worth it! It's a pretty complicated tool, but for a good reason. You can customize it however you need, and it can implement with almost any other software you use! Very, very flexible.

While NetSuite is powerful, it can feel overly complex for everyday users, especially when simple tasks require deep configuration or workarounds. Some reporting and UI behaviors (such as recalculation delays, limited control over certain system fields, or inconsistent filter behavior between reports and saved searches) can be frustrating. There is also a noticeable learning curve, and changes that seem small on the surface often require admin involvement, scripts, or workflows. Finally, performance and transparency around background processes (like project profitability recalculations) could be improved to help users better trust and understand what they’re seeing.

The things that can be improved in using Netsuite are the options to give when editing. Example is when editing a posted payment, it doesn't give option anymore to check the Credits Tab. When editing a posted payment as well, the amount you input in the 'discount' column and 'applied' column will be different so if you click EDIT, (for example you want to edit the Memo or date in the upper part), you need to edit the whole thing if you input different amount in discount and apply. When posting as well a lot of invoices with Chargebacks, there's a time that it was loading for so long then the work we've done was refreshed so we need to do it again.

Our financial budgeting and reporting capabilities in NetSuite are quite limited. We have to resort to using external tools like Excel for these tasks, which is cumbersome since the data is already in NetSuite. It would be nicer if NetSuite had more flexibility in reporting.

I think NetSuite is hard core in many aspects and not flexible enough. Like saved searches, reports, the database behind only allows connecting two directly linked records. Also, the home dashboards are limited to certain customizations.

Building reports is complicated, especially when we want to join data. The system as a whole is often slow, with pages taking 30 seconds to load. The UI uses too much space on unnecessary information and hides common information behind menus that need more backend calls. We have to pull data out to Excel or OneDrive because of weak reporting and awkward document management. We had to contract with third parties for small improvements and sign up for an ACS contract to ease setup difficulties. There are hundreds of fields with overlapping names, making it hard to search. Accessible documentation and the ability to run SQL or SuiteQL queries would make generating reports easier.

There's a lot of features within NetSuite that I'm not aware of. It's well hidden within the tool, and sometimes it's difficult to find some of the new features just because of the layout. If there's going to be a new launch, they should drop us an email, an easy-to-read email with what the latest launches are and how we can benefit from them. Maybe a short video as well to show how some of these features can be used. I think that'll be really useful.

I believe NetSuite could have created a more user-friendly UI. The one in place usually relies on a steeper learning curve that usually takes time and effort for the client to adopt effectively and rapidly.

NetSuite could be improved in system performance as certain processes or large reports can sometimes be slow with high data volumes. I'd like faster processing for saved searches and reports, background processing for heavy tasks, and improved dashboard refresh speeds.
NetSuite's WMS didn't meet all requirements for us like RF Smart does. NetSuite doesn't have a built in TMS. Sometimes the data centers go down too long.

There are some connection limitations in terms of which fields can pull onto which forms. For example, a field on the Sales Order may not be available on the Item Fulfillment. In many cases, creating a custom field is the solution, and it’s usually not a big deal. However, there have been a couple of instances where certain field information simply wasn’t available, and that has led our company to require a custom solution.
Their pricing structure is something that has made few our customers question their judgement on taking on Netsuite as ERP. The frequent revision in prices causes distrust.

The manufacturing and MRP modules feel underbuilt for anything beyond simple discrete BOMs. We ran into a BOM direction error that caused fractional quantity issues in wafer singulation work orders — a basic lot sizing problem that required manual intervention to diagnose and fix. Lot and serial traceability doesn't work cleanly out of the box for multi-tier supply chains; we had to build workarounds using the Inventory Numbers saved search instead of a purpose-built genealogy view. The UI is dated and navigation is non-intuitive — finding the right saved search or transaction type involves too many clicks. Onboarding and support are heavily consultant-dependent, and documentation for advanced features like SuiteQL or Workbook formula syntax is sparse. No meaningful AI or intelligence layer exists in the modules we use — no demand sensing, no anomaly detection, nothing that reduces planner cognitive load.
Design engineering is fragmented which can cause issues if you need to update more than one module, each one is completely different.

Honestly, there isn’t anything I genuinely dislike about NetSuite. Like any powerful platform, it can take a bit of time to fully unlock everything it offers, but once you’ve found your feet with it, that flexibility and depth quickly become one of its biggest strengths.
It would be great to have sufficient introduction or training done prior to implementation or at least support videos that can help on every module.
I have been using it for almost a year, and I haven't encountered any serious issues.
It is few native NetSuite limitations like cannot update the subsidiary on vendor customer and employee record if any transactions are coded.

We have run into some things we have put in for improvement such as the ability to create invoices from sales orders, which is what our customers would like to see. Our customers only see invoices paid, but a sales order can be processed with several invoices, so they would like to know which invoices are created from which sales orders.
Bills don’t have a clear connection or related records to item receipts because we use Ramp. It’s PO to item receipts then PO to bill, but there’s no connection between item receipts and the bill.
NetSuite does not allow me to automatically download my bank transactions as I bank with A Credit Union.

There are some areas where improvement is required. Due to the depth of features, new users may face a learning curve, which can affect ease of use during the initial phase. Implementation timelines may extend when advanced customizations or multiple integrations are involved, and simplifying setup and navigation would improve user adoption. Customer support response times can sometimes vary based on issue priority and support plans, and faster resolution would enhance the overall experience. Additionally, system performance optimization and more intuitive configuration options would help ensure smoother daily usage and better utilization of features.

I think there are some glitches that could be improved. There is a glitch when looking at payments that has an extra scroll bar and makes it tough to see. If this bar was removed (as there already is one) that would help, as you have to get it in the right position to see anything.

I gotta be honest, the thing I hate the most is that the UI feels super old and cluttered. Its like it was designed in 2010 and they just kinda slapped some new features on top. There are so many menus, sub-tabs, and fields crammed onto one page, it gives me a headache.

I would love to have the ability to process payments for customers in NetSuite. We are currently doing it manually. This is a feature that QuickBooks has that NetSuite doesn't offer. It wasn't easy to set up NetSuite initially. There were a lot of setbacks.

There are some parts about adding custom fields and making them as important as things like customer that just isn’t possible. For example, we lease trucks and tracking by VIN is everything, and we can't go into a specific VIN and see all that has happened to it like I could with a customer. Easier ability to create fields that have their own forms and tracking throughout the system. I have been trying to fight this battle for years. It was tough but once it got running it worked. It can be difficult to adjust to new processes.

It can be difficult to navigate around until you learn the ins and outs. Sometimes the native language (naming of fields, results, etc) is hard to follow with some things being extremely finite while at other times there is a sense of vagueness. It's almost as if the developers are counting on users being like-minded to them.
The only downside is that it's not pretty - aka not intuitive to use. There's always a period of struggle when someone new starts, and they usually can't find their way to what they need by accident.

We are currently using NetSuite primarily for Accounting, CRM, Revenue Management, and Billing. It supports our financial operations with strong reporting and automation capabilities, while also helping manage customer relationships and revenue processes efficiently. The integration between these modules provides better visibility across departments and enables smoother coordination between finance and sales functions.

Our version of NetSuite has been customized so heavily that it’s hard to find answers to our questions online. I wish there hadn’t been so many customization options during setup, because those choices ended up backfiring on us.
Some of the web store design in the site builder could be a little easier to use, especially with linking to pictures, PDFs, and information. That would be helpful to make the website design easier.

Sometimes the level of customization is so detailed that you don't always know what it is that you need to make sure you've included. Or what isn't included. You have to ask it very specifically and make sure you've included everything that you want.

From a usability standpoint, the UI can be clunky at times, especially for newer users who don’t work in it every day. Simple tasks sometimes take more clicks than expected, which slows down workflows.
At first, it was quite challenging to get familiarized with NetSuite, but as time goes by, it's easy.

I think if AI is added to help center it can be very much helpful. For example if I can write a prompt to the help center AI chat and it directly responds me the correct API endpoints and methods that I can use for the solution implementation.
The customization is nice, but I do find it to be complicated and require outside assistance, or a lot of time, to set up.

I don't always feel support (both ACS and ticket submissions) are on the same page as what we're trying to achieve. I also think we have a knowledge gap in the system with ours users, so having some sort of complimentary Oracle representative learning would be ideal. Specific to our company, rather than the webinars.

Accounting for construction requires some customization in addition to configuration. Customizations are required for PSR reporting and Percentage of Completion revenue recognition. That requires either a SuiteApp or customization. Basic setup was easy, but construction functions were a bigger challenge.

Some fields are very difficult to locate in the Saved Search builder, and there are simply too many options to sort through. In the native Income Statement and Balance Sheet reports, it would also be helpful if the left side could remain static so I can more easily review data across later accounting periods, locations, and other report views.

NetSuite's user interface could use a significant modernization. Loading times often lag, and the navigation structure isn't intuitive, which means I spend more time than I should digging through menus to find specific tools. The platform is powerful but highly technical, making it difficult for new users to pick up quickly and even causing experienced users to lose efficiency due to its non-intuitive layout.

They sold the product to us and then getting them to help with the integration and meet deadlines has been like pulling teeth. I'm incredibly frustrated and have been trying to wrap up an integration since January 1st!
I find processing large files in SuiteScript within NetSuite to be challenging.

I personally used it during my audit of a client. It was the client platform for a very small company (revenue < 1M). From a technical standpoint, it felt laggy and overly complex for a company of that size, and it was a bit uneasy to work with. Personally, it felt bit less easy to work on than the other ERP systems.

The UX isn’t the best compared to competitors, and support often feels very robotic. It also seems like you frequently need to be on a support contract (internal or external) to get the help you need.
NetSuite can give some pretty generic error codes. Sometimes connecting to it can be really hard. We have a SuiteQL project going on right now that doesn't work well.

I am newer to using the product. I would like more resources(maybe zoom call meetings/learning?)available to continue to learn new use cases I could incorporate into our company. Specifically from the analytics side of the business. I am attending a lab session on February 19th 2026 in SLC that should provide more resources for this.

The out of the box setup just doesn't work for our company and you essentially need a degree in programming or a background in NetSuite with admin level access to modify. Or you can pay thousands of additional dollars for a managed services team to assist with build out. Most of which, is not a quick process and takes all your hours for what should be simple requests. The timing delays for page loading, overly complex reporting features and many other levels also make learning to utilize NS to its full potential very difficult for a novice.
Sometimes it takes many clicks to navigate between different modules. Support can be difficult and 3rd party consulting can be costly.

The SuiteApp deployment sometimes gives errors without clear information, causing us to debug in the dark to figure out what's wrong with the error objects. For example, I got an error on a custom form with an unhelpful message: 'An error occurred during custom object creation.'

One of the main drawbacks I’ve experienced with NetSuite is that the user interface feels dated and a bit clunky. Although the system itself is powerful, the visual design and overall navigation can come across as unintuitive when compared with more modern platforms. As a result, it can slow down day-to-day work, especially during repetitive tasks. Or when training new users who expect a cleaner, more streamlined UI.

There are a few things like support response times - while NetSuite has a strong customer support community, some users have experienced delays in response times. Cost considerations: For smaller businesses or startups, the cost of NetSuite can be a barrier to entry.

Not being able to allocate based on percentages per vendor, not having an easy way to maintain complicated routing rules. SuiteApprovals is terrible, having to find workarounds for bundles because I or other developers cannot edit or fix it. The initial setup was not easy at all; Oracle did a terrible job of discovery, requirement gathering, and now we're in a place where we have to do a reimplementation.

I think the implementation process could use some work. Initially, it's very difficult to get it to where it needs to be. It should be tailored per industry first, then enhancements can be added after the initial implementation. Also, the initial setup was not easy due to internal factors and a bad choice of consulting company.
I wish SSO worked better with Microsoft. The initial setup was very complicated, but we had a team of experts help.

ARM is really clunky, too many layers to (Arrangement, Element, Plan - why???) Reporting isn't the best either too many limitations on table joins for the out of the box searches and reports. Custom SQL reporting isn't really an option unless you purchase the warehouse. Even using SuiteAnalytics Connect it doesn't work with Tableau times out, tables not available, etc. Analytics reports are too limiting in the amount of data they can handle. Amendments on Subscriptions aren't as they were advertised and are also too limiting, no control over the amount of the adjustment which doesn't work unless you are also using NetSuite as your CRM/Quoting system which most companies don't. Often times NetSuite is the receiver of the data, so ideally it could be configured to receive amounts in these circumstances allowing a bypass of the NetSuite proration calculations.

I find the customer service lacking and feel it could be improved. The initial setup of NetSuite was problematic due to receiving friendly but uninformed consulting help from the NetSuite team, which led to many delays and setup headaches.
I find the dashboard build-up, cash-flow management, and forecast in NetSuite lacking. Additionally, the lack of two-way integration with NSPB is a downside.
The time that things are processing, sometimes I feel that is a lot of time spent on loading pages and reports

Its flexibility can sometimes lead to complexity if governance isn't strong. Reporting could be more intuitive for finance users, and licensing costs need to be actively managed as the organization scales. Finance reporting would be more intuitive if standard reports were easier to customize without saved searches, with more flexible filtering, drill downs, and layout controls that are closer to how finance teams work in Excel.
The saved search is taking too long time. I need to refresh the saved search a couple of times.

I have heard a lot of users complain about ease of use. I think making the UI more streamlined and having more useful help documentation would be great. Support can be incredibly difficult to work with as there are very long delays between when a case is submitted vs. gets a reply, and the replies are often not helpful.
The biggest drawback of NetSuite is its high cost and complex pricing structure. But on the big scale, it's good enough

I find the interface management could be improved. NetSuite requires manual review of scheduled imports and manual intervention for rejected records. It also deletes interface results after 30 days, making it easy to lose information. It was somewhat challenging to understand the underlying concepts of NetSuite, especially how the relationship tables are shared by customers, vendors, and employees, which took a while to figure out for the best data setup.
Sometimes, the system can be slow, especially when handling large amounts of data. The user interface, while generally good, can feel cluttered and overwhelming at times.

The payment automation feature needs improvement. There's an issue where, for instance, Expedia, which is a vendor account for all our entities, can only be assigned to one entity even though all entities pay Expedia. Additionally, setting up the vendor process isn't explained well enough, with insufficient information on properly setting up a vendor on Payment Automation for check or ACH.
The only negative I've run into is the lack of reporting around Amortization Schedules

Sometimes the reports aren't as customizable as I want them to be. I can't run a consolidated trial balance by subsidiary for the audit period. Custom fields cannot be used for financial reporting. I feel like since NetSuite and Suite Projects Pro are both Oracle products, they should be more compatible, but they feel like two completely separate programs.
Support system, I understand the support may take time for an issue, but it's to long even for a P1 issue. NOTE: This has been improved recently.
I find the amount of data to integrate from other systems and the speed to load massive amounts of data problematic. Also, setting up NetSuite was easy for financials but hard for ERP.

I do not like the bill approval process. There is something wrong with the process to where if the intial bill is entered wrong below the threshold then edited to be above the threshold it skips the approval process. I also think the reports you can actually use could be better management reporting is not a 100% option using solely NS because of limited reporting functionality

The system has a steep learning curve, especially for warehouse staff who just need to move stock quickly. The interface can feel slow and overly complex for simple day-to-day tasks like picking, receiving, or adjustments. Changes and customisations often require admin knowledge or external consultants, which adds time and cost. Training is essential, otherwise productivity can drop.
I found the documentation weak when I started. It should be organized by task, not features.

The size/quantity limit of only allowing up to two sizes is a very limiting factor for us. The reporting features have also been limiting. Fixing the online support forms to integrate better with the fields used would be nice. KPIs should be easier to setup as well as having up to 200 items to see instead of just the top 50 items. The initial setup was a nightmare. We used NetSuite's team and they were clueless. We had to find an outside developer, purchase a whole new instance and it still took us over 18 months to go live.

The support team is not responsive. My account manager often does not respond or takes a long time to get back to me. This makes me want to look for a different or better account manager to handle my account and relationship. Also, the initial setup of NetSuite was somewhat painful, largely because of poor support.

I think the banking module in NetSuite is severely lacking, especially when compared to other software currently on the market like QuickBooks, which we switched from. It's a huge learning curve to use the banking module, and features like bank match data, bank reconciliations could all be greatly improved with better automation. Also, the initial setup was awful, and we ended up needing a third party to help implement the software.

The price of NetSuite is quite high, and editing any of the customizations requires additional fees and support. The initial setup wasn't easy; we had a consultant to help, but they dropped the ball and did not complete the training and integration. Also, no tools are directly integrated with NetSuite.

I find it frustrating that the demand and supply plan is generated by single item, not by a Supplier. If I select a supplier, it should create a demand and supply plan for all the listed products against that selected supplier. Also, the opening and closing stock should be clearer.

I believe that users should not be asked to directly specify the accounting account in various scenarios—for instance, within Inventory Adjustments. At that stage, users are typically not accounting experts. Instead, I have created a custom record type called "Adjustment Reasons," which automatically contains the specific account I wish to use.

The native reporting functions in NetSuite aren't great. It's clunky to build custom reports and the exports don't look great or executive. This requires the use of exporting TB data to Excel and building custom reports or using connectors like Solutions7 to automate the builds. The initial setup was a bit of a challenge.

What I like least about Netsuite is that when searching for a bill or vendor, you have to enter the exact information. This becomes challenging if the team responsible for creating profiles makes a typo or includes an extra space, as even a small error can make it impossible to find a vendor.
I don't like the fixed asset module because it's complicated, unintuitive, finicky, and pricey.

Bulk loading of information is slow and clunky. Overall, the system has a lot of nickel and dime type add-ons that are not included in the base package and significantly increase cost. Almost everything that you do with NetSuite or the support team tends to increase the overall cost that comes with the charge - they love marking things as "out of scope" and requiring an add-on cost. NetSuite charges significantly more to use any sort of integration compared to other software like Salesforce.

The e-commerce suite would need to be updated, and local adaptations, particularly in France, sometimes take too long to be implemented in the standard. The support of an integrator is essential to perfectly adhere to French accounting standards, for example, for mirror entries or proper management of payment terms. Scripts have been necessary.

The training course availability. I always want to improve my skill to write a report or create an ad hoc search. Right now I can only use very basic search functions and have to ask my IT people to write a report or search for me, which is inconvenient.
I feel like it performed comparably to other CRMs. That said, I always felt the interface didn’t look as nice or as clean as some others, like Salesforce or Hubspot.

I wish there was some way of NetSuite telling me 'this field hasn't been used in 5 years' or 'this field isn't connected to anything else, no search or reports.' There are a lot of workflows, scripts, and fields that are not used and make the environment junky. Notifications for unused, duplicate, or overlapping flows would be helpful, like if one workflow is just counteracting another one. Specifically, I want notifications and real reviews of systematic processes.
Sometimes NetSuite is slow. If you customize reports with lots of filters, reports tend to take a very long time to generate.
The only drawback I can think of is that there isn’t a way to search invoices or payments by dollar amount.

In a Journal Entry, it's difficult to discern whether there's an attachment or not. The drilldown capabilities in a financial report are not as specific as expected; you have to try several times before you get the data you want. It has been a problem to integrate with the bank feeds. The initial setup of NetSuite was terrible, as the recommended healthcare partner was not the expert we were led to believe.

The integrations with outside providers could use improvement, and the OAuth2 process is a bit annoying because it requires weekly refreshing. Setting up machine-to-machine tokens is also harder than it should be, needing a custom third-party application, which makes it more complicated.
The navigation. The path is too long, which made my team to take time to learn

It does take time getting used to especially the user interface as it's very much a legacy system build in terms of design. I've never needed to use the support as it's pretty simple after a demo or two.
Not user friendly like at all, and have to pay for upgrades to do just about anything is just plain insulting to it's users
I wish there was an AI chatbot where end users can query their company data from one chatbot window.

The interface can feel a little outdated compared to newer tools, and the learning curve in the beginning can be challenging for new users. Once you get comfortable, though, it’s a very capable system.

The implementation process was slower due to the team being in different time zones, facing internet issues, and having illnesses. My own medical issues and part-time schedule also contributed to the delays. We tried to use NetSuite for employees to request POs, but it doesn't allow them to enter the account code, which takes away the reason I purchased additional licenses. The cash flow statement is too minimal. Being the only person working on the implementation on my side was a bit challenging, despite this being my third time implementing NetSuite.

I would like a more flexible way to get reports. The customize options are confusing; for example, if I need a P&L by vendor and try to customize the P&L, the field 'name' appears 100 times and I don't know which one it is.

It was too rigid for our use case. It is in a place where smaller companies can transition to ERPs, but for many there would a large learning curve during this transition. Some features are somewhat hidden in layers.
It is difficult to navigate, it has lots of glitches, very slow, limited to customizations and it's more out of the box product and when you do updates, you override some of our customizations.
NetSuite WMS has been a struggle to customize, and the out-of-the-box functionality doesn’t work well for most operations.

NetSuite has poor reporting functionality, they want you to purchase an additional budgeting & reporting package from them. Due to the level of customization, there were some complexities during go-live that created additional errors.

The UI is a bit clunky, it doesn't have the easiest learning curve, learning is a bit overwhelming and can be hard to figure out where to start, and there are too many extra modules that require additional money. It's also very hard to tell when a module is included in your current subscription or will cost more money to add on.

I've experienced issues with the staging playground, known as Boomi, in NetSuite. There's some delay and certain jobs don't run as they should or as scheduled. It could be better at identifying and reporting logs back to third-party applications. Connecting NetSuite more seamlessly could be improved. Also, the analytics and insights could be presented in a better way for both nontechnical and technical users.

I wish there were more training guides that were easy to print and give to operators that can't sit in front of their computer for hours to do training sessions. It was difficult because the organization heavily customized a lot of things instead of trying to work within native NetSuite where possible.

Certain features don't work or aren't easily customized to the users wants or needs. Additional support and services are costly add-ons and because of its 'adding on' over time it's inconsistent in many ways. Also, it can be difficult to find answers sometimes. I'm constantly being tried to be sold add ons. Reporting and viewing things for certain teams is difficult in NetSuite.

If something happens, it’s very difficult to solve the problem. In my experience, customer service isn’t really helpful, because they keep telling us to contact a different department to address our issue.

NetSuite can be complex to implement and customise, often requiring specialist partners and ongoing administrative effort. Licensing costs and incremental charges for modules and users can also make it expensive over time.

At times, I find that Netsuite support is not particularly helpful, especially when it comes to major functionalities that should work but don't. Instead of addressing these issues promptly or offering alternative solutions, support often categorizes them as Enhancements. Unfortunately, some of these enhancements are never implemented and can remain unresolved for as long as four years. This is an area where I believe Netsuite really needs to make improvements.

The .CSV import feature is very finnicky and tough to get right. As a beginner to NetSuite, the learning curve is mostly trial-and-error and can be frustrating with lack of guidance or understanding what went wrong.

The reporting can be a bit challenging, especially for some of the additional modules such as Fixed Asset Management. There is also not an easy way to manage billing adjustments for simple things, such as sales tax, as the revenue module is tied to all invoicing and credit activity. Lastly, I found the implementation to be a bit of a struggle but I believe that was more of an edge case of our implementation manager missing the comprehension of a few of our use cases. Further challenging this is the rigidity in how Netsuite uses system GL accounts to perform certain functions that didn't fit our old system. This brought challenges in data loading GL activity to our FP&A platform.

Typically, there's about a three-month window before going live, which involves constructing everything according to the specific industry and vertical needs, and this can be very intricate. If there were a way to simplify this process, particularly during implementation, it would greatly relieve some pain points. Often, organizations encounter issues from the onset because data is input incorrectly or lacks proper structure, which isn't a flaw with NetSuite itself but more to do with how businesses rush to get it operational, likely due to its high price point and the complexity of the sales cycle. Simplifying implementation would prevent many issues, ensuring data is correctly placed and clean from the start, ultimately solving numerous problems down the road.

This forces Accounting to manage another step after sync to either modify the bill coding and line item to add the amortization schedule or have an entirely separate process to establish amortization schedules that will not be attached to the related and originating Prepaid Bill. I think native features must be supported for them to be a recommended add-on provider.

However, while NetSuite is incredibly easy to use and integrate niche business requirements with, it's not always a straightforward path to get there. When we start a new development project we are sometimes left ping-ponging between wanting to keep the project within the NetSuite environment and building a new website to have more control. "How can we keep this on NetSuite instead of building a new website", is the question we ask 9 times out of 10. NetSuite does not support access to things like the browser's DOM, nor can we add custom HTML/CSS/JS to anything beyond an inline HTML field. - There was a time where I built a whole form, complete with tables, image uploads, radio buttons, text fields, etc. ALL within a singular InlineHTML form field. This is not a NetSuite supported interaction, and required quite a few workaround "hacks" with Ajax and jQuery to be able to gather information from the html field and send it back to NetSuite to record the data in a record. Another project, we needed to build a NetSuite "assistant"-like page that would pull data from different records and then display cabinets that met the criteria selected. This was a primarily front end facing project to show to customers. Naturally, it would be easier to keep this project within NetSuite given that is where the data is. But because we did not want to recreate the mess that was the previous project mentioned, we opted to build a new website complete with it's database to prevent querying the NetSuite DB too often which could be unreliable and slow. However, now we have a new website that sales staff need to constantly be on and we need to maintain it as well. Perhaps if there were a new script type, call it an HTMLlet or a ReactLet, that allowed interaction with the webpage more freely and could receive and send data to and from NetSuite natively we could use NetSuite as a standalone application builder. Additionally, one thing I would truly love and would make my role 1000% easier is if there were more tooling available to us! An official VS Code extension to have a SuiteScript 1.0 and 2.x/2.1 linter would make a world of difference in preventing bugs.

Lack of Accountability & Misleading Practices A major red flag we overlooked early on was their refusal to allow meeting recordings, claiming they wanted to "protect proprietary functionality." This excuse falls flat—numerous public demos exist online. The real reason? Avoiding accountability for unmet promises. Without recorded proof of commitments, we had no recourse when deliverables fell short. Failed Implementation & Broken Commitments Fixed-Cost Implementation Scam: They charged upfront but delivered only half the solution, leaving us with broken workflows and no support. Held Hostage for Basic Deliverables: To complete the original Scope of Work, they now demand payment for their "Advanced Customer Service" team (Philippines-based at $300/hour). System & Performance Issues Unreliable Platform: Frequent outages, sluggish browser-based performance. Inexperienced Implementation Team: Even NetSuite’s on-shore team lacked product knowledge. Many offshore contractors had language barriers, yet we were billed premium rates. NetSuite WMS – A Mislabeled Disaster Not a True WMS: Missing basic functionality (e.g., real-time inventory tracking during picking/packing). No Performance Metrics: Tracking picker/packer efficiency costs extra. Zero Visibility: Once an item is picked, you cannot locate it (e.g., on a cart, in a tote) until shipping completes—a fundamental WMS failure. Ship Central – A Broken Shipping Module Inventory Discrepancies: Failed to deduct stock during shipping, leaving items stuck "in limbo." Clunky, Error-Prone Interface: Deviating from the workflow requires 10+ admin steps to undo. Manual printer selection for every single shipment. Slow Processing: 30-60 seconds "thinking" time per label, plus another 30-60 seconds to print. Reprinting Labels is a Nightmare: No intuitive reprint option—forces digging through logs, which is impractical in paperless environments. Final Warning Do NOT invest in NetSuite WMS or Ship Central. Their products are incomplete, inefficient, and poorly designed—costing more in fixes and lost productivity than their value.

I am writing to express my extreme dissatisfaction with the service and support provided by NetSuite over the past year. Unfortunately, my experience has been fraught with numerous issues, ultimately leading me to decide not to renew our contract. From the outset, the implementation of NetSuite was handled incorrectly, causing significant disruptions to our business operations. Whenever we encountered an issue, the standard resolution offered was to purchase an additional module, which felt more like a sales strategy than a genuine effort to resolve our problems. Moreover, the pricing structure for essential features such as payroll and profit and loss by jobs was not made clear upfront, leading to unexpected costs that strained our budget. Throughout our time with NetSuite, I found that no one seemed to have the necessary expertise to fix the issues we faced. Despite numerous requests to speak with supervisors, I was consistently told that our problems could be solved by spending more money, which was both frustrating and unacceptable. Compounding these issues was the frequent turnover of our account manager, who changed four times within the year. This lack of continuity meant that no one had a clear understanding of our account history or ongoing issues, resulting in repeated explanations and a general lack of progress in resolving our concerns. It is evident that the primary focus was on receiving payment rather than providing effective solutions and support. This approach has left us with a system that does not meet our needs and has caused considerable disruption to our operations. Given these circumstances, I cannot justify renewing our contract with NetSuite. I hope this feedback will be taken seriously and lead to improvements in your customer support and service delivery for the benefit of future clients.

Everything after the sales process completed. I have never written a product or software review, but feel compelled to warn small business owners about NetSuite so they don't wind up in the same situation. In my 22 years as a small business owner I can easily say this is the BIGGEST mistake I have ever made and by far the worst purchasing decision. Unless you are large enough to have a full time, very technical resource completely dedicated to the implementation process, and time to allow your full team to completely self train on the system you will have problems, no matter what they promise you during the sales process. We dropped NetSuite after the 1st year as we STILL WERE NOT FULLY IMPLEMENTED 1 year into the process (and had to pay the full license fees for that full year even though we didn't have a fully functional system the entire time!) The package we paid for the implementation process provided horrible support. It was an inexperienced team of two people who always had to get back to us with any questions we had (2-7 days for an answer) - never knew the system beyond just repeating what was in the generic training videos (which are horrible training videos) and processes. Zero project management, we were just told 3 weeks before our 'implementation term' ended that their time is up and even though our system wasn't implemented they are done - no warning or notice. No reminder at each 'monthly' meeting that they only have x weeks left of their support (yes, once per month is there connection point to get you implemented, that's it). Only options at that point were to pay more fees to add on consulting packages - which still took another 3 months just to get the payroll module (another up charge) implemented. Renewal pratices are unethical. No renewal contract was provided until 15 days before inital 1 year contract ended - thinking that in such a short time we would be stuck and have to renew as there would be no time to flip elsewhere. I said no, I won't renew. We had been having to 'double enter' everything into QBs the entire year since our NS system was never fully implemented - and we decided we would rather just stay with QBs. The account rep then told me well too bad, in your original contract it states you have to provide me with 30 day notice if you will not renew so you have to renew whether you want to or not. I argued how could I provide 30 days notice that I don't want to sign a renewal contract when my renewal contract wasn't provided until 15 days prior to end of term - no response from rep at all. Instead, they just automatically renewed me against my rejection and I received the renewal invoice and was charged the full next year amount. They were FORCING my renewal even though she knew I was incredibly unhappy with the system. It was the biggest 1 year $100k+ mistake I ever made ($40k to them, easily another $60k in internal labor costs) - walked away and will never have anything to do with NetSuite again. If you are a small business without full time staff to completely manage the implementation effort, run away and don't get sucked into the sales promises - none of them panned out. What I projected as a $30k per year expense (which was a stretch for my small business) based on the sales team's promises quickly turned into so much more to actually get everything I needed once implementation was rolling. And the unethical renewal practices are completely not okay.

The most challenging thing about NetSuite is customization and configuration management. The platform is very much configurable, and it's scripting extension capabilities are wide-open. However, it lacks key features to fit into a traditional change management scheme. Unlike some of its low-code competitors, it does not expose all of its configuration knobs and switches as externalizable metadata. So, while we can easily manage script versions in traditional source code fashion, we cannot similar connect the configuration change dots. So, when a developer addresses a customization story, we can associate script changes in source control the same way as we do our bespoke applications, but all the related (and necessary) declarative changes, such as custom fields, workflow definitions live in NetSuite alone. We can deploy scripts using automation, but config changes have to be applied by manually by administrators and history tracked with our own config tracking documentation scheme. The other biggest issue, perhaps more of a missed opportunity, is the CRM module. It's poor user experience for Sales staff, and the relatively weak case management features make it difficult to support - which is a shame because having the CRM and ERP on the same platform solves a lot of big ticket challenges for us.

Also, I have not had a lot of time to play around with the querys/reports, as I am somewhat restricted, but I am curious if reports can be set up to auto run on a schedule with specific criteria which may change from week to week.

I am not thrilled with NetSuite's pricing model. They offer deep discounts off list price to get started with your subscription and then over-time back-off the discount , with the goal of you eventually having to pay the full list subscription price - typically increase is 10% annually plus a reduction in your discount (i.e. start at 50%, reduced to 45%, 40%, etc). The general argument is that they are continuously improving their solution and there is no overt charge for upgrades, beyond adjustments to their list price and discounts given. Regardless, the value (price vs. quality) is certainly there. Biggest concern is now that Oracle owns NetSuite, their pricing model will become incomprehensible and they will find a way to make you pay for the same solution over and over again by repacking and calling it something else, like Fusion.

The downside is the learning curve and lack of training on the front end before Going Live. Users can sometimes take a class two or three weeks before using the system and forget everything they learned by the time it is time to use it. Take a day for a dry run on the weekend with fake transactions in the sandbox. Practice makes perfect! The investment into a fantastic system and skimping when it comes to training is foolish. If you are going to invest, then invest in training the people responsible for the day-to-day operations in using the new tool and understanding how it impacts the transactions that follow.

SuiteCommerce does not allow us to use different units of measure in the web store, forces us to use only the primary sales unit. We manage our sales and purchases in 1,000s but customers need to buy in full truck load pallets, which are different sizes (quantity of pieces per pallet) depending on what kind of items are on the pallets. Multiuser access is costly. Online access requires hosting on an application server, which can be expensive; it can also be complicated, which is a turn-off to unsupported/casual users. The bank-transaction import/matching feature is a little lacking; it doesn't learn rules quickly and isn't very flexible. If you're quick at data entry and/or your data tagging needs are complicated, you may just decide never to use it. Repairing bank reconciliations that have been thrown off by changes or deletions is more difficult than it is in QBO. Would love to see the QBO feature that shows you what went wrong and offers you a way to fix it without undoing and redoing everything implemented here. The audit trail is a little less functional in QB Desktop than it is in QBO. Lacks a real inventory (costing) system, though third-party solutions are available. Lacks the ability to store percentages by which costs need to be allocated; this means that you're better off doing complicated allocation data entry outside the program and then importing it; it also hobbles the program for allocated budgeting and forecasting. Lacks safeguards against importing the same data multiple times. From a database perspective, the Name field is poorly defined, which can be a problem if you need to report on multiple dimensions (I've run into this problem particularly with non-profits that need very detailed reporting). When QB Desktop crashes, it crashes hard, but support is generally very good.

PAYPAL DUPLICATE TRANSACTIONS: I couldn't figure out why I had up to three transactions in Quickbooks for every one PayPal debit card transaction. Frequently, there would be two debits and one credit in Quickbooks. It turns out that there is no workaround to prevent this from happening, even in the new software I'm using, but the new software clearly showed me what was happening with my account, which made finding a solution easy. (I'm currently in the process of switching accounting software.) MISTAKES: Quickbooks also does not allow you to easily undo mistakes. Sometimes, you simply cannot. :-/ MOBILE APP: I get an error every time I try to upload a picture of a receipt using the mobile app. This is why I dinged "reliability." RESOLUTION: With the PayPal issue, Quickbooks required that I unlink PayPal and connect it a certain way. This deleted all of my work with my previous transactions, such as categorizing and uploading receipts. NOT FOR BEGINNERS: As someone who catches on quickly, QuickBooks was NOT intuitive (unlike the new software I'm switching to). Even after figuring out my PayPal issue, QB seems like too much of a pain to continue with.

There's not much I dislike about QuickBooks desktop. Maybe a few things are: 1) I cannot have more than one A/R or A/P line item in a transaction. 2) Aside from manual journal entries, I wish that QuickBooks would default the debit line item first before credits after a transaction is entered. 3) There are plenty of controls, but it might be nice to have it so that a staff accountant can input the transaction, but the manager has to approve it. Even though QuickBooks is user friendly by comparison, if you don't understand accounting, there's a very good chance you'll screw up your books. That's no fault of QuickBooks - it's user error. Maybe this is why QuickBooks has a stigma with accountants in larger companies - "QuickBooks is for neophytes, not sophisticated financial professionals like myself," they say. QuickBooks has the reputation for only being suitable for mom-and-pop companies, not larger companies with employees. I've had accounting supervisors tell me this multiple times at different companies. When I press them for specific reasons why QuickBooks does not work for their company, many times they cannot think of a reason why - it's just the fashionable thing to say. There may be some legitimate reasons why a company needs to upgrade from QuickBooks Pro. For example, you cannot consolidate financial statements from multiple companies (but QuickBooks Enterprise can). Or if you need a full blown ERP. But before you make the switch ask yourself: DO THE BENEFITS OUTWEIGH THE COSTS!! If you switch to a Frankenstein software (like MSGP), you might get new functionality and integration from other departments, but now you LOST the streamlined ease and basic functionlity that QuickBooks offers. Maybe you should consider having SEPERATE software applications to keep track of what it is you are trying to incorporate into your accounting system. Or use a company like Level Field which can lay on top of all of your software applications (CRM, ERP, accounting, marketing, etc) in real time. Talk to my friend Jude Samson to learn more - http://www.levelfield.com.

I have a client who uses Intuit GoPayments. Although this product is advertised as working seamlessly with Quickbooks Desktop, this is not the case. Transactions are frequently missing and Quickbooks Desktop requires that each and every transaction have an invoice created for it. I do not need invoices, because the payments are made at the time that service is rendered. This is not the case with Quickbooks Online - on QBO, you do not have to create invoices and Intuit GoPayments plays quite nicely with QBO - you can just click to issue a sales receipt. Unfortunately, this isn't the first time that a feature that exists on Quickbooks Online has been missing from the Desktop version. I have concluded that Intuit is making an effort to move away from the Desktop version in order to focus their efforts on QBO.

While QuickBooks Desktop Enterprise offers impressive accounting capabilities, there are a few areas that could be improved, especially in today’s increasingly remote and cloud-based work environments. The most significant limitation is that it's a desktop-based platform, which means you are tied to a physical location or network setup unless you invest in third-party hosting solutions. For finance professionals or teams that work remotely or across multiple locations, this can create accessibility challenges. In contrast, cloud-based alternatives offer more flexibility for real-time collaboration and access from anywhere. The user interface, although functional, feels outdated and less intuitive compared to more modern platforms. For new users, the learning curve can be steep, especially if they are unfamiliar with the structure of enterprise-level accounting systems. Some features are also buried in menus, making them harder to discover without prior training. Additionally, the licensing and upgrade costs can be a barrier for smaller businesses. Annual renewals and add-on modules (e.g., payroll, inventory) can increase the total cost of ownership over time. In summary, while the software is reliable and feature-rich, its lack of cloud integration, outdated design, and cost structure may not be suitable for every team, especially smaller or fully remote organizations.

While QuickBooks Desktop Enterprise is feature-rich and powerful, it can feel a bit overwhelming at first—especially for those new to accounting software. The interface isn't as modern as some cloud-based alternatives, and setup can take some time. However, once you get the hang of it, the level of control, customization, and depth it offers is absolutely worth it. The learning curve turns into a strength because you’re working with a tool that truly grows with your business.

Quickbooks enterprise promises many features, but anything beyond invoicing is highly laborious and rarely works. Do not rely on Quickbooks for anything beyond normal accounting. Basic tasks are made difficult by horrible UI decisions with little to no control over customization. We are continuously shocked by the decisions their devs and product managers make with usability and functionality. Something as simple as creating or editing invoice templates is dreadful and riddled with bugs. Editing price lists feels like some sort of sick joke. Sales order fulfillment is about 10% of the way to something useful. Multi-user fails consistently. Most issues must be fixed in single-user mode bringing everyone to a halt. Integrations out of the box are almost non-existent, mainly due to Quickbook's ancient and unmaintained architecture. On top of everything wrong with the software, support is useless and often worsens matters. Always be prepared to run through the same 2 hour checklist, including uninstalling and reinstalling before support will even answer basic questions. I have yet to have support assist in fixing an issue correctly. I have resolved what I could on my own through lengthy research. Some problems have been unresolvable, and QuickBooks shows no interest in assisting. These are significant software-breaking issues, like Quickbooks failing to generate a pairing code for a scanner device, rendering WMS entirely unusable.

Quickbooks can be finicky. The system they use does not like having locked-down folders or rights. Every so often we will have problems with Multi-user mode or with the services running properly. There were many issues with it, starting with the downloads from the bank. You would have a full month reconciled, a month with about 1000 transactions in it. Then magically after doing that it would just unreconciled an item that was in that reconciliation. This meant having to go back and do the full reconciliation again. In some cases back multiple months and re-reconcile up to 3 months again after doing it, then maybe repeat that adventure again in the same month. Then it would download duplicates of transactions, not letting you know it was a potential duplicate. The search functionality to try to find things absolutely was atrocious. If you owed staff money for reimbursement, or to withhold funds from their cheque, you could not have a staff record also be showing as an account payable or account receivable, so you'd have to create a second record for them as one of the others, yet you couldn't use the same name as it said it was a duplicate. I tried to call the help desk, but you needed a subscription to "ask for help" to talk to a live person, so left to try to scour the forums for answers. Trying to migrate it to Quickbooks Online was done with a wing of hope and a prayer and took me 20 hours on the phone with 9 people in 4 different departments to actually get it done. Every person I talked to told me different things. On top of this, you pay for the privilege - at a whopping $300 a month vs the $36 a month for the same amount of users for their online version. The Payroll portion of Quickbook Enterprise - well you have to use Telpay as it was their supplier. Telpay at the time (and 2 years ago still didn't) have any way to actually reconcile the money that you sent them with what is paid out - unless you want to pay them $50 an hour to see if they can actually reconcile the records if you're out. Then we had to also use a remote server to have it set up as we needed, which was yet another subscription service and when you generated your T4's they landed in some mystery folder that you had to call them to try to find and they would have to actually move your T-4's for you, then you would have to do your own upgrades, but in order to do that you had to ask for more space as there was enough space for QuickBooks, but not enough space for you to do the upgrade until the old one was removed. While I do understand we have a higher volume of transactions than the average Quickbooks users, the issues we encountered were beyond what could be reasonably expected.. The cheque printing could never get it quite right, I could go on.. but that is more than enough

QuickBooks Online has deliberately removed the ability to print IRS-compliant 1099 forms with boxes, a feature that existed for years and is still required for many established workflows. There is no technical limitation here — the data exists, but the form layout is intentionally suppressed to push users into paid e-filing. This forces businesses and accounting firms to either pay extra, manually re-enter data into IRS PDFs, or use third-party services. This is a clear regression, not an improvement. It increases cost, wastes time, introduces error risk, and creates unnecessary compliance confusion. Removing core functionality to coerce add-on purchases undermines trust in the platform. If you rely on predictable, standards-based accounting workflows, be aware that QuickBooks can and will remove them without notice.

The reports: I’m much more familiar with the Desktop reports than the Online ones, and I’ve found the reports in QBO to be less user-friendly. I’m getting more comfortable with them month by month, and using the reports has definitely become easier as I learn my way around. That said, the filter options still aren’t as strong as the options in Desktop.

I believe that, because they’re an industry standard, they try (and most often succeed) to nickel-and-dime users for all types of services that, in my opinion, are highly overpriced. Yes, you can use their payroll services, inventory services, barcode integrations, and many other add-ons, but the prices are outrageous compared to non-QBO options. The things I dislike most—and the main reasons I stopped advising my clients to switch to QBO—are essentially twofold. First, the subscription price has skyrocketed over the last 5–10 years. The basic QBO plan is so limited that even very small businesses (2 employees, <$1M in yearly revenue) will quickly outgrow it and end up needing one of the much more expensive subscriptions. On top of that, the subscription pricing for additional services is also much, much higher than competitors. At the end of the day, you can easily pay twice as much (or more) than what competitors charge for equivalent or even superior products. Second, the customer service and support department has suffered tremendously over the last 5–10 years; perhaps in an effort to increase shareholder value or profit margins. Getting customer support now is a giant hassle—and it’s expensive, too.

I'm reaching out to express my serious frustration with Intuit’s customer service and policies. I need to downgrade my subscription from Advanced to Simple Start—I have no interest in paying $235 per month for a plan that’s beyond what I need. However, my attempts to make this change have been met with unacceptable roadblocks. The system is designed to push users towards costly options, making it nearly impossible to downgrade without a battle. This kind of tactic, where customers are compelled to jump through hoops just to adjust their plan, is incredibly frustrating and feels exploitative. It’s shocking that I was even asked to pay in advance before a downgrade could be processed! In addition, the quality of customer support is unacceptable. When I reached out on 11/08/2024 for assistance, I was transferred from one agent to another, none of whom seemed equipped to help. One agent, whom I could barely understand, hung up on me, seemingly at a loss on how to resolve my issue. When I tried using the chat support, I was met with the same lack of competence and assistance, only to be told I would need to wait until Monday since no one was available to help over the weekend. It’s astonishing that Intuit, a company of this size, would allow a situation like this to unfold, effectively preventing customers from managing their subscriptions easily and efficiently. Forcing customers into upselling conversations when they simply want a downgrade option should not be standard practice—it feels misleading and manipulative. I'm posting this complaint publicly to make others aware and to urge Intuit to reconsider its policies and improve its customer service standards.

It's clear that QuickBooks has no regard for the impact their actions have on their customers' credit ratings and overall financial health. They merely reiterated their policies without taking any responsibility for the misinformation provided by their own support team. This lack of accountability and transparency is unacceptable for a company handling sensitive financial data. I strongly advise anyone considering QuickBooks to think twice. Hi, We are a company delivering physical sport coaching sessions with 800 players coming for sessions every week. We have 4 terms per year so around 800 x 4 = 3200 sales and every sale can be multiple services and products. Customers pay by PayPal, Credit card and PayNow (Singapore) and bank transactions and sometimes combine different products and multiple players’ payments (siblings together for discounts, etc.) We have 2 major problems with QuickBooks: 1. No functionality to send sales receipts for bank transactions. 2. Bad working Match functionality for bank transactions No functionality to send sales receipts for bank transactions. QB has a sales receipt functionality which adds one or more transactions to QB. However, when a bank transaction comes in though the bank links, we like to send a sales receipt directly based on the transaction and combine several transactions into one sales receipt. QB lacks this functionality totally and there is a super complicated solution on YouTube which we are not going to use as QB should make our lives easier. As this is a very simple functionality to build in QB, the question is why it is not available where competitors have this functionality built in. Is QB still upgrading their system or just leaving it as it is with no improvements anymore? Bad working Match functionality for bank transactions QB has a sales receipt functionality which adds one or more transactions to QB. However, when a payment comes in the match must be completely and exactly the same as the sales receipt, which is impossible as my customers might pay with different payment methods and multiple transactions will add up to one sales receipt. Also, QB takes the sales receipt date as transaction date which is a big mistake for a system as QB. The sales receipt will mostly be sent after the payment date when we have confirmed the payment has been made. We do not send invoices and we have a website where players and parents register, select, and pay for the services and products. We are sending the sales receipt out of QB after receiving the payments. At this moment we are not able to receive the payments in accounts that are linked to QB as it is impossible to match transactions and sale receipts. Another option is to send sale receipt out of other systems and do the bookings in QB based on the transactions, which is double the work for us. What we need is a simple option to list more transactions in the match which do not have to match the sales receipt but, for example, the name of the customer only, depending on a filter. We honestly do not understand why QB does not have better match functionality as it is now just impossible to match without manipulating the sales receipt to an exact match, which is a ridiculous amount of extra work and goes against all accounting principles.

I dislike the inability to transport data into Turbotax - This function routinely fails, and the resolution is to enter the info by hand, negating the app's main functionality. Furthermore, the app is difficult to use in the following areas: - Sending tax info to TurboTax -- The main reason I subscribe to Quickbooks is that it makes it keeps track of tax information that may be imported into TurboTax. This saves time because I don't have to input tax information by hand; however, the import functionality has not worked for at least two years in a row. The first year I was told it was because I tried to file too early after receiving an email that I was eligible to file. This year the issue is unknown. - Invoicing -- It's difficult or impossible to delete an invoice, change a sender's info, or otherwise update the invoice. It's also not possible to see what the invoice you're sending looks like before you send it, and the invoices seem to get stuck in spam filters. I'm not sure what email they are sent from. Also, I often need to send invoicing info to multiple people and the invoicing system only provides a BCC, not a CC. - Mileage -- The mileage tracking system is cumbersome, and it does not save info or make entering round trips easy. The auto-tracking feature assumes all mileage is for business but only a fraction of my mileage is. - Performance -- Reviewing transactions is sluggish and often lags and registers screen presses out of sync with screen updates, leading to incorrect selections. - Support -- Help isn't there when you need it. I used the help feature from TurboTax to receive a call right away when the import failed; however, they do not support QuickBooks and sent me to QuickBooks support, which was not open or accepting calls. I only found out they were unavailable after navigating the automated support menu that attempted to walk me through the steps leading to the failed import. - Exporting to TurboTax -- At the risk of belaboring the point, exporting does not work. Not only does it fail, but you are only allowed one attempt at exporting. The solution given in the error notice and by the TurboTax staff is to enter the information by hand, which is unacceptable.
