My customer paid the invoice I sent him, he received a receipt, money was transferred to my Quickbooks account, and after a day or two of processing, they were deposited to my bank account. The customer received services he paid for, and a week late I see that the whole transfer amount was deducted from my bank account. I didn`t receive any explanation at all, I didn’t get any email from QuickBooks whatsoever. I had to call them and spend 2 hours on the phone to hear that cause of the currency difference they had to refund the amount to a customer. And they just suggested that I contact the customer and ask him to pay again. I thought that if you pay monthly fees for a payment system, then once this system accepts, processes and deposits the payment it means that it accepted, processed, and deposited the payment. How come they can come a week later and say that there was an error and now you on your own have to get your money back? They definitely do not protect either the seller or the customer. They protect just their business and all “your bank account is safe with us" is such a joke. How do I know the same situation won`t happen to me again? If the system processes the payment and deposits it to my bank account, how can they just take my money back and say that there was some system error? Sounds like a robbery and a reason to call a lawyer to me

I have owned a small contracting business using Quickbooks for several years. I recently decided to set up Quickbooks Payments to allow my customers to pay their invoices via credit card. There was a processing fee that came with each transaction, but I decided that the convenience of quick payment was worth the cost. I was utterly mistaken. The first credit card payment made to my business was processed in a reasonable timeline. The second payment, made a few days later, was held up by Quickbooks for review. They requested additional information be sent to them (bank statements) to verify my account. This request was completed within a day. Since that point nearly three months ago, I have called Quickbooks on four separate occasions to get some explanation as to why my payment was being withheld. Every conversation I had with their service team took over an hour. No one could give me an actual explanation, but they always guaranteed me that the payment would be released within two business days. It has been almost three months, and I still have not received payment or an explanation. The lack of communication, direction, and, most importantly, the payment has caused me to lose all faith in Quickbooks. These actions are causing my company to be on the brink of dissolving. Please, take my advice and avoid Quickbooks at all costs. Regretfully, A disappointed business owner.

Their customer service stinks. Their systems and processes need serious improvement. I had a client pay me 2 weeks ago and I am still waiting on these funds. I have had to pay out of my pocket to cover the inventory. Let me just walk you through this nightmare: 1. Client makes payment 11/23 2. Within 5 minutes sent the requested information back. Spent over an hour on the phone with them trying to figure out what is going on. This information has already been provided to them. One person doesn't know, another person says that it is because my passport was not scanned correctly? Why didn't they ask for my passport again in this request then? They backtrack on that and say nevermind that isn't the issue. Just be patient. 3. Funds should have been released on the morning of 12/5. Chatted with someone who said that I didn't submit my documents right away and that was the delay. Not true. They confirm they currently have everything they need and to wait a few more days. INSANE! I have been waiting almost 2 weeks at this point. 30 minutes later I get an email saying they don't have any of the information that I uploaded and they are requesting it again. They updated the requested document date, which on the system shows the review day being pushed out another 2 days. 3. Chatted with THREE different agents. Each said they would transfer me to the correct department. Each time the chat ended and nothing happened. This is my first few weeks experience with QB ^ Coming from Zoho Books I have had literally 0 issues. We are a corprorate gifting platform and in order to purchase inventory for clients I need a fast deposit. Luckily we were able to front the inventory for this order but I no longer have trust in QB. We will likely be closing our account. I cannot imagine this happening to a client where I couldn't afford to purchase their inventory upfront. It would absolutely ruin our reputation and be beyond embrassing. I cannot trust them to be our merchant servicer. I literally have no idea when our funds will be released. Quickbooks has created a closed ecosystem that all but forces you to use their payment merchant services (no native Stripe or other 3rd party processor integrations). I recently did a QBO deployment for one of our businesses and enabled online payments for a new invoice that we generated. QBO had no problem adding the online payment link to the invoice, which the customer subsequently used to make payment. This is where the fun started. Only once the client payment had been successfully received, did I get a notice from QBO that the deposit to my account had been put on hold until they could "verify" my account / business information. Fair enough, I understand the requirement and had gone through similar processes with several providers before. However, after submitting our (very straightforward) documentation requirements, I was informed that Intuit had denied our merchant application. No reason given. No opportunity to rectify. Just denied. In addition, the customer payment that triggered the verification would be held for 270 DAYS! as a "security deposit". Here's the response from their 'support': "Alright, A bit of bad news. Intuit closed your account as a business decision. These decisions are final unfortunately. So with the payment you have processed. You would have to have the customer dispute the payment with their bank or card company. Then take the funds another way or it will sit on the account as a security deposit for 270 days. Then you would have to reach out 271st day to receive the funds. I am sorry for the inconvenience and I do apologize. Unfortunately, Where Intuit is considered a low risk processor, and sole proprietary company. They don't have to provide notifications for closures or give the reason for closing the account as a business decision. I am sorry." So I'm left with 1 of 2 choices: Ask the customer to try reverse the payment from their end (not a great look) and try collect payment online through the clunky, non integrated payment processor options in QBO. Or go cap in hand to Intuit in 271 days time to ask if they wouldn't be so kind as to release the (significantly sized) customer deposit that decided to unilaterally hold onto with no information, clarity or recourse. In summary, be careful of deploying QBO if you want to collect payments online. I can't recommend this solution for anyone beyond a 'mom & pop' style business.

We have had some really bad experiences with tech support over the years. I am the oldest digital native you can imagine. I am 59 and had a computer when I was in high school in 1980. I have spent hundreds of hours on customer and tech support over the years. I was certified in Apple Server management. I am fluent in Adobe, MS Office, HTML. I programmed DBase 4 and Filemaker. I was an early adopter of CheckFree before Intuit bought them out. I have run Quicken in several iterations, Quickbooks desktop and Quickbooks Online. My daughter is a forensic accountant in law school. So I speak with authority when I say that Intuits customer is about the worst, on average. With QBO you pay for tech support as part of your expensive subscription. What this means is that you can talk to an offshore person working from their home, which was true even before the pandemic. If you need a call back -- which I have on several occasions -- there is a minimal chance you'll get one. Their VOIP is unreliable. It couldn't call my phone (believe me everyone else can). They don't call back when they say they are going to. You can never rely on the front-line folks. ALWAYS escalate to a supervisor to reduce wasted time. They won't use email to contact you if they can't get VOIP to go through. They have a proprietary interface (in the same vein as zoom screen share) which is a tech support nightmare in and of itself. Thankfully, the software is not usually buggy, but if you need help, it won't be there for you, and accounting software is complicated enough that an online forum is not the way to solve the bugs that do exist. And did I mention YOU'RE PAYING FOR THIS SERVICE? I would NOT go back to the desktop version under any circumstances. However, if you can find a different package, I strongly urge you to consider it first, second and third, and if all else false you may be, like so much of the world, stuck with this horrible monopoly.

I have used Quickbooks products for 15 years now and have been happy with everything they've put out up to a couple of years ago. As they started coming out with new products like QB Cash I was quick to sign up and start using them. They promised great features that I was excited about; however, that's when everything became a dumpster fire for me. They started getting rid of features on their mobile app, one of my other businesses used QB Self Employed, and they started getting rid of features there as well. QB Online began to have a lot of glitches, wouldn't load at all, would freeze, would quit randomly as I was building an invoice and had to restart, etc. And I tried multiple devices, browsers, etc. The app also barely worked anymore to the point I stopped using it all together. I tried to sign in the other day, and it literally took 10 minutes just to try and sign in to my account from my phone because of loading times, pages crashing, etc. Now for QB Cash, I actually lost money through that. I tried to make a transfer, and the system glitched, and it didn't go through, but then they charged me a fee because of it. When I tried to get help, no one could help me. I got transferred to 3 different departments, and none of them handled QB Cash. One person FINALLY was able to tell me it's not even handled by QB, it's by a third-party bank which is fine, but even the customer service and online portal was supposedly handled by them. So I called them, and they didn't know what I was talking about; they didn't even know what QB Cash was. So I kept calling people and FINALLY got to someone who said they handled QB Cash, and I shared my issue, and surprise surprise, she said she couldn't help either. But at least she was able to submit a ticket to her support team and said she would get back to me - that was more than six months ago. After that, I was charged TWO MORE FEES all for something that wasn't even my fault. So I tried to get all of my money out of QB Cash and close the account, but for two weeks, it was glitching every time and wouldn't let me. So I called in multiple times, and still, no one could help. I finally figured out a work-around with PayPal and got my money out. Then I had to pay back all the fees because they were about to put me in collections, and I didn't have time to spend hours fighting with people who just kept telling me they couldn't help. Then when I tried to cancel, two accounts went just fine, but my third has been a mess. I canceled it on my own through the settings like my other two accounts, but that didn't work. So I spoke with someone to have them cancel it for me, and to my surprise, they not only canceled it but refunded my last payment! That was two months ago. Then this week, I got a notification that QB tried to charge my card. Thankfully I froze that card so that they couldn't charge me. So I called in and was assured the account was canceled, and I wouldn't be charged, but of course, today, I was charged. And had to spend another 30 minutes getting that fixed. I could go on and on, but bottom line, I've spent hours and hours on chats and on the phone with QB support because their product has become so difficult to use with all the technical issues, and their customer serivce has been terrible except for a few very good agents. On top of that, I signed up at $35/month, and over a few years, it was raised to $53/month, AND their payment processing fees have gone up considerably as well. So I switched to WaveApps that is FREE and has more reasonable payment processing fees and does everything QB does - without the glitches.

We migrated from the desktop/on premise version of QuickBooks to the QuickBooks Advanced online version several years ago. While it has been nice to login from anywhere, the functionality has been far more limited with the online version. Simple things like creating great estimates or tracking our customers by state and rep have been painful or impossible to get right. The estimates are extremely limited in their functionality. We wrote a special connector to bring the customer contact data from our CRM to QuickBooks, and special templates to try to make QB estimates work, but we could never get them formatted in a way that was sufficient. There were common fields like line-item discounts that it simply did not support. Worse, there was also no adequate helpdesk assistance to be found. The system also requires the use of special fields for some simple things that were standard in the desktop version. Segmenting customers by state or sales rep were some of those items. And, now that I want to downgrade to a less expensive version with more limited licenses, from my current $1,920 per year QB fees, I’m being told that the lower cost version doesn’t support these special fields, so I’ll lose all that data. These fields should be part of any accounting software at any price! Lastly, the support is just plain painful. The people are always nice, but they rarely have more than the most basic answers. That’s mostly because of the limitations of the online version of QuickBooks. And, forget about ever talking to development. They are located in a blackhole somewhere. You can email them, but you will almost certainly never hear back. Overall, I’d call Intuit QuickBooks a--formerly wonderful--failed product. I’m actively looking for ways to abandon it all together. I do not recommend it. The value proposition has soured.

They have no phone support, not an option. If you already have a QBO expert available to you, you are golden, but for anyone who may occasionally require support, you are out of luck!

Support can have a long wait time. It really depends on the day/season but on a bad day you could be on hold for an hour. Thankfully their help knowledge-base is very useful. We often find the answer to what we needed while on hold. The credit card processing add-on is a little on the expensive side if you use it very little. It has a monthly fee whether you use it or not, so it's not cost effective if you sell through credit cards seldom. On the other hand, if you're constantly charging customers with a credit card it is more competitive than our previous 3 credit card processing companies. We actually stopped using QBO for card processing because of the monthly fees multiple times and every single time we ended up coming back to them because the ease of use justifies the expense to us. We rarely use it, though, so it's really not geared for us or companies that, like us, mostly get paid through checks. Lastly, the number of allowed users is a little limited. They just expanded on this but even now it's not enough for what we wish. Ideally we'd like to give limited access to technicians and sales reps so they can generate quotes. Online access for multiple users is easy to set up, but the program is often slow to respond, even over a good internet connection. Lacks the Advanced Search of all transactions, regardless of type, that makes QB Desktop so much more flexible and easier to use. QBO also hides advanced options (delete transaction, undo reconciliation, reverse transaction) behind multiple layers of menus. I understand this to be part of an effort to make the product friendlier to non-bookkeepers, but I use the Accountant version, and these functions are still hidden under layers of GUI. I far prefer the capacity that QB Desktop offers to strip the GUI down to a workspace and a full selection of drop-down menus. Lacks a real inventory system. For that, you will have to use an outside app, and that is going to raise the cost. It is also near impossible to create your own solution for missing features, as Intuit has obscured the underlying database mark-up language. For instance, if you are a non-profit that need to apply customer and class tags to multiple transaction lines for later reporting, it is far quicker and easier to do that in a spreadsheet and import the transactions. But you can only DIY that in QuickBooks Desktop and with its publicly accessible IIF mark-up language. If you use QBO, good luck finding (and paying for) a third-party app to fill this need. I also find that the labeling of transactions (e.g., Expenditure is more prominent than Bill) and the prominence with which the bank integration/transaction matching feature is promoted to conceptually favor cash accounting practices, which are not preferred or even legal for most businesses. But they're intuitive to casual users, and the so the interface leads casual users down garden paths that an outside bookkeeper will eventually have to fix before tax (or worse, audit) time. I've earned plenty of money fixing the problems that QBO leads casual users into, but I wish it didn't encourage them into those problems in the first place. I also find the GUI distracting and obscuring in its own right. So much screen real estate is spent, even in the Ultra Compact display mode, on graphics that are supposed to be user friendly that I can never see as much actual financial information as I would like on one screen. Also, the bulk-editing functions in the accountant version of QBO are hobbled compared to those in QB Desktop.

Prepaid expense module where you have a lot of issues because our accounting changes so regularly.

One downside of Sage Intacct is that it can have a bit of a learning curve, especially for new users who aren’t familiar with cloud-based accounting systems. Some advanced features and configurations may require training or support to fully utilize.

One thing I dislike about Sage Intacct is that some processes are not as intuitive as they could be, especially for new users. Certain tasks require several steps or navigating through multiple menus, which can take time to learn. While Sage Intacct is a powerful tool, the interface can feel somewhat outdated and unintuitive compared with newer cloud platforms. Training the team and learning all the modules we needed also took more time than we expected, which made the overall ramp-up less efficient.
I honestly don’t have anything I dislike about Sage Intacct. Overall, it’s been a clear improvement in every way compared with the platform we were using before.

The multi currency functionality is a little limiting, but there are work arounds to make everything work. I think they could stand to improve that functionality. I also wish there were more options native to Intacct rather than having to implement a third party.
I would like more interactive functionality within Sage Intacct's core system, instead of needing it as an add-on for recording.

I'm trying to think of what we could improve on. We have an integration with a third-party company, and I'd say the most difficult part is the order entry. It is not as easily manageable and editable when you're doing the auditing process as when you're in bills.

I use imports in Sage Intacct and initially they would send you an error message when your import had errors, but never described what the error was. My only input on imports would be that they would have a preformatted import that allows unnecessary information to be removed and only necessary information added. Initially, we struggled because we didn’t understand the dimensions and how it would work for us because we were going from one entity to 28 and everything was new to us.

What could be improved is the advanced consolidation module, specifically with how new it is. We're still working through some quirks as an early adapter. The intercompany relationships are confusing and not straightforward. It would be helpful to have more feedback on setting up intercompany by industry when the environment is transformed or implemented.
It seems to me that there should be more connectivity with other third parties, for example, with banks, in order to have updated information every day to reconcile accounts more easily.

One drawback of Sage Intacct is the steep learning curve for new users. It can also get expensive, especially when you need add on modules, and some features still require help from technical support. On top of that, reporting customization and the overall interface can feel complex and, at times, less userfriendly.

Custom report writing is a bit cumbersome and takes some learning especially when it comes to understanding reporting groups. When adding new accounts the user needs to be aware that the new account may not be properly mapped to one of the groups and could therefore put reports out of balance.
Continue the great work of adding new features. Never know when I may need to use it one day.

It would be better to have a field that allows adding multiple bank details. We often receive requests to transfer certain payments to a secondary bank account. Currently, to process such payments, we need to change the existing bank details, which is not ideal.
Poor integration with third-party tools. It doesn’t sync smoothly with other software unless I rely on manual workarounds, and the API capabilities feel limited compared to competitors.

Implementation can be lengthy and resource-intensive, especially for multi-entity or otherwise complex organizations. It took a long time to understand the reporting piece and I still have trouble with the custom reports that have so many levers it can be confusing. I'd like to see the ability to have bank rec approvals similar to journal entry approvals. It takes lots of planning and thought to set it up most efficiently.

I think that maybe some of the buttons could be labeled better as to what they do. Sometimes it is difficult to understand what does what. Sometimes it breaks it down that it's on line 20, other times, it does not.
The only thing I’ve disliked about Sage Intacct in the past was the ongoing disruption with the bank feeds, but over the last few months it seems like that issue has been resolved.
Sage Intacct needs proper training, and I think it has challenges with other systems integration, the sync process needs patience, and there are many system glitches. I think it is in its complexity.

I think the biggest improvement I would love to see is the ability, when in an invoice, to see how it was coded the previous month. This works when adding a bill but not when uploading a bill. I have to go out and run a report and then come back to code the invoice.
Integration with Paycom. We want the journal entry to go straight to Sage Intacct.
The only area that needs improvement is the loading time when pulling custom reports. It tends to be more time-consuming, although this isn’t the case for the regular AP and AR inputs.
That we must go through a third party for support and often do not recived quality responses. Often we are refered to watching videos or reading the help canned responses.

I've been waiting for this question. I have a huge issue with Sage support. I submit a number of tickets and they rarely get resolved. The typical Intacct customer service response is formulaic. They more often than not steer you towards articles that you have to read. These articles do not help. Customer support is the biggest thing. If you have a good support team, you can accomplish anything you need. Sage is limited and they won't let you do anything without paying for it. It's hard to get anyone on the phone to workshop issues or ideas without paying money. Account Managers aren't equipped with the knowledge to resolve issues.

I struggle with the purchasing workflow. If someone is off sick or on holiday leave, it's very manual to change the PO approver department, rather than being able to put them on 'holiday' and getting it to go to someone else. The initial setup was quite time-consuming, and the test system wasn't quite usable to be able to test properly. There were a lot of things Sage Intacct didn't tell us about setup, which caused a lot of problems which we are still having to fix two years later.
It's not a huge issue, but if there was a way to maybe make it easier to set up some of the reports.

I'm not loving Sage Intacct so far. The implementation has been very difficult and slow, which has slowed down our daily operations. I would have liked to be fully set up by now. The integration for Procore was working well but stopped after a while and is stuck. Reporting has been hard to get correct. Setting up the program has been a roadblock, and the initial setup was very, very difficult.

Some functions feel overly permission-dependent, which can slow you down. For example, with cash transfers, we’ve run into situations where you can’t edit a transfer once it’s created unless you have top-level authority, so the only practical option is to reverse and redo it. That works, but it adds extra steps and creates noise that we then need to explain internally. Also, certain more advanced use cases aren’t as intuitive without training. Consolidations are the main one, Intacct is clearly capable, but it’s not something you can just “figure out” as a team without consistent setup and some structured guidance. Same for project budget management, it can work, but it’s not immediately obvious how to set it up in a way that’s simple for everyone to maintain. If you don’t invest in training and standard processes, different people end up using workarounds, and you lose some of the benefit of having a system like this.
I don't like that Sage Intacct is too Americanized. Language and accounting terms in some areas of the program are very USA-centric and not relevant to the rest of the world market.

There aren’t many options for allowing multiple account selections in the general ledger when viewing customized reports. The filtering and customization features also need improvement to make it easier to narrow down and work with the accounts I need.

I'm frustrated with the updated webinars involving W9s and 1099s this year. Last year was easier. Also, it seems like the Tax Bandit upload always kicked back something, and I needed help from my supervisor. Changing a vendor from MISC to NEC from one year to another was also a problem.
I found the training a bit tedious. It felt slightly repetitive, with not much variation throughout the core learning modules.

Some workflows and automations aren't as intuitive as they could be, and the UI feels a bit dated in places. Workflow setup could be simpler and more transparent, and the UI would benefit from fewer clicks for common tasks and more consistency across modules.
Nothing to dislike in Intacct. All the features available help me to do my day-to-day activities very easily. So I would strongly recommend this application to my friends.

It is not the most user friendly and has taken a bit of time to get familiar with (and I've appreciated the support we've had from our support provider when trying to change the less typical bits of configuration)

I find the usability and learning curve of Sage Intacct to be the biggest challenge. Some workflows aren't very intuitive, requiring significant training for new users to feel comfortable. Creating custom reports can be frustrating; even small changes like adding a new dimension or adjusting filters can break the report, and it's not clear where the mistake happened, making it more time consuming than expected. Performance issues and responsiveness of the platform has been an issue for us. There is significant lag in even logging into the system sometimes taking over a minute to sign-in. There is not enough value in the 'standard' modules. Everything seems like it is an upgrade they are charging you for (e.g., better budget and forecasting, additional API calls). Sage is not easy to use. Typical daily workflows for accounting staff are not smooth and are require lots of extra clicks. Even though the import feature in the GL is great when it works, it is not ideal when it comes to helping us identify errors in our imports when there is one. Some parts of the UI could be modernized. You can't see the entire general ledger account when editing a journal. I can't see what is in the box unless I leave and go to view or go deep into the cell.

The implementation process. They were partnering with NPact at the time of our implementation. We had 4 different representatives throughout the process. I do believe they are using a different company now... Npact did not listen to what we needed. When we met with the new rep and had to explain our business processes again, we were told what our previous rep advised us on wasn't possible and had no effort made to rectify the situation. They did not show us fully how to use the features needed that were included in our package to be trained on and also marked off that they imported our customer records on the implementation checklist but did not do it until we requested multiple times. We were also handed off to Sage support without signing off that implementation was complete and only found out after emailing our representative about issues and being told they could not help us and we needed to contact support. While Sage Intacct offers many advantages, there are some drawbacks to consider. First, the platform may have a steep learning curve for users without prior accounting software experience. Despite its user-friendly interface, mastering the advanced features and workflows can require significant training time. Additionally, its pricing can be higher than some competing solutions, especially for smaller businesses, as it’s generally priced based on usage, modules, and customization options. Another potential disadvantage is that while Sage Intacct offers strong integrations, setting up these integrations with third-party applications can sometimes be complex and require additional IT support. Finally, although Sage Intacct is powerful, smaller organizations may find it overkill for their simpler needs, as it’s primarily designed for mid-market and larger enterprises.

1. You never know what's not going to work next. Every month Sage has a new issue when trying to produce AIA style billings. 2. They claim there is one standard cost code for all jobs, this is beyond a lie. Every single job in the system has its own set of cost codes that you have to manage. We have around 400 actual cost codes but have to manage over 800,000 thanks to how they have this set up. 3. The GL interface is extremely clunky and slow for user input. They don't allow you to copy the job or the entity down so you have to input on every transaction line. Their default setting doesn't work unless ever line item is going to the same project because every job has it's own set of cost codes. 4. Researching in Intacct is slow and difficult, if you've used Sage 100 in the past this will be a huge down grade 5. The Interactive Custom Reports are a joke. Who designs a report tool that you cannot display what information was pulled in the report? What job is this, what time period, any cost codes excluded? You'll never know once you hit print. 6. Naming conventions are not consistent; there is no consistency between the names used on the Object list and what is used in the Custom reports which makes trying to build out your own reports difficult. Which is a major issue as the system comes with no stock reports.

My biggest frustration with Sage Intacct is how poorly it integrates with QStock and certain aspects of Salesforce. Since both are widely used inventory and sales management systems, I expected a seamless connection, but instead, integrations are unreliable. There are frequent discrepancies for item quantities on order, on hand, on hold, & available where data between the two systems does not match up, causing discrepancies between purchase orders, inventory counts, and customer orders. This often means our teams have to manually double-check data, which completely defeats the purpose of having an integrated, automated system. Another issue is updates between Sage Intacct and QStock don’t always push through properly or in a timely manner, which can lead to delays in tracking received goods, verifying stock levels, and processing customer orders. This makes it harder to ensure accurate purchasing decisions because I cannot fully trust the inventory data is correct. It also lacks seamless two-way communication, meaning I sometimes have to re-enter or adjust information in both Salesforce & Intacct systems manually. This not only wastes time but also increases the risk of errors in customer account records. If Sage Intacct had a more reliable integration with QStock and Salesforce, it would make purchasing and inventory management much smoother. Right now, the inefficiencies force unnecessary workarounds, which is frustrating when trying to maintain accuracy and efficiency in operations. Lastly, it is frustrating that some fields are unavailable to add as columns in personalized views.

Invoice management is spread across multiple modules and needs to be made more lean. The Accounts Receivable and Order Entry modules both hold the same invoice and offer different capabilities, which sounds good in theory (more options is rarely bad), but unfortunately it creates too many opportunities for information to appear in one module but not the other. A favored reporting option may only be available under one module and not the other, leading to data integrity issues. If you're thinking of using the Contracts module for managing your SaaS customers, one word - DON'T. It does not aggregate subscription information within the UI, forcing the reader to look elsewhere for information they should be able read at the source. You also have to jump over way too many hurdles to place restrictions on date ranges for any subscription changes. The module is simply not built for the modern subscription model; it harkens back to a time when perpetual licenses and maintenance agreements were common.

Ensure that all necessary tasks are completed before entering into a contract with Sage. It is crucial to have the Sage team address your specific system requirements before committing to their services or consider exploring alternative providers. Customization in Sage is similar to NetSuite, so be prepared to pay a premium and negotiate a favorable contract upfront if their out-of-the-box solution does not meet your needs. It can be surprising that some Sage representatives may not possess a thorough understanding of their own system and may suggest solutions that are not entirely effective. If you engage their Professional Services team, be aware that it may take a week or two to begin working with them, and the quality of their work may range from unsatisfactory to average, at a $250/hr rate. Unlike Microsoft, where you can find a variety of skilled developers at different price points that can deliver, locating affordable Sage developers can be challenging. The high hourly rates are not the primary concern, but rather the dissatisfaction of paying for partial completion of a task. Unfortunately, the customer success team at Sage may not be very helpful, as they might lack knowledge about their own platform. Therefore, do not rely solely on their customer success team or Technology services team to deliver. Hindsight, I would have approached this entirely different or went with an entirely different platform had we known what we know now.

The biggest drawback is the limited flexibility compared to on-premise versions, especially when it comes to deep customization or highly specific business processes. Extensibility options exist but often require workarounds or adherence to SAP’s strict framework. Additionally, the learning curve can be steep for new users, and initial configuration, including data migration and integration with legacy systems, can be complex and resource-intensive.

The system can feel complex and comes with a steep learning curve for new users. Customization options are also more limited than in on-premise versions. Implementation and migration can be time-consuming and costly, and occasional performance or integration challenges may still come up, especially when working with legacy systems.

Some aspects of SAP S/4HANA Cloud could be improved. Customization is more limited compared to on-premise ERP, which can be restrictive for complex or highly specific business requirements. If I’m being honest (and fair), SAP Cloud ERP is powerful—but it’s not without trade-offs. Here’s what I dislike most: Limited customization (especially Public Cloud) You can’t freely modify the system like in ECC or S/4HANA On-Prem. Many changes need to follow SAP’s standard processes, which can be frustrating for businesses with unique requirements. Dependence on SAP’s release cycle Mandatory quarterly/bi-annual updates mean you must constantly test changes. While innovation is great, it can impact stability if not managed well. Higher total cost over time Subscription fees, integration costs, and reliance on SAP BTP for extensions can make it expensive in the long run—especially for mid-size customers. Complex integrations Integrating with non-SAP or legacy systems often requires middleware (BTP/CPI), adding complexity and skill dependency. Steep learning curve for consultants and users Fiori, extensibility rules, and cloud restrictions require a mindset shift. Traditional SAP consultants often struggle initially. Not ideal for highly localized or customized processes Some country-specific or industry-specific needs still require workarounds or side-by-side solutions. In short: SAP Cloud ERP is great for standardized, forward-looking organizations, but it can feel restrictive for companies that rely heavily on deep customization or legacy-heavy landscapes.

What I dislike most is that the **Clean Core restrictions can feel quite limiting**, especially when you’re used to the flexibility of traditional SAP ECC. Some common requirements often need workarounds through BTP or APIs, which can increase complexity and effort. The **upgrade cadence and strict standardization** can also be challenging for teams not fully mature in cloud development. Overall, it sometimes feels less flexible, even though that trade-off is what keeps the system stable.

Like any software solution, SAP S/4HANA Cloud has some potential disadvantages that businesses should consider before implementing it. Here are a few potential disadvantages of SAP S/4HANA Cloud: Cost: SAP S/4HANA Cloud is a subscription-based service, which means that businesses must pay a monthly or annual fee to use it. This can be a significant cost for some businesses, especially if they have a large number of users or need to use a lot of additional functionality. Customization: SAP S/4HANA Cloud is a pre-configured software solution, which means that it may not be possible to customize it to meet the specific needs of every business. This can be a drawback for businesses that have very specific or complex processes that require a high level of customization. Implementation: Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud can be a complex process that requires significant time and resources. It may be necessary to invest in training and consulting services to ensure that the system is implemented and used effectively. Integration: While SAP S/4HANA Cloud is designed to integrate with other SAP and non-SAP applications, it may still be necessary to invest in additional integration solutions or custom development work to connect it to other systems. Dependency on the cloud: SAP S/4HANA Cloud is a cloud-based solution, which means that it requires an internet connection to function. This can be a disadvantage for businesses that rely on the system for mission-critical processes and may not have consistent or reliable internet access. Overall, it's important for businesses to carefully evaluate the potential disadvantages of SAP S/4HANA Cloud in the context of their specific needs and requirements before deciding whether it is the right solution for them.

One thing that could be improved is the learning curve. For new users, it can take some time to fully understand where everything is and how different processes work. Also, certain tasks require multiple steps or clicks, which can feel a bit time-consuming at times. Sometimes the system can be slightly slow when loading reports or large data sets. Improving navigation for specific functions and making some processes more streamlined would make the overall experience even better.

In general, it’s very positive, but I’d say that it’s interface is not very user friendly, which makes it hard to use and find things sometimes. Related to this, the usability is not very intuitive, but that won’t keep you from using it, it’ll just make it a little harder.

One challenge is that certain workflow can feel overly complex, especially when handling exception or non-standard invoices. Also the reporting function is powerful, but creating highly customized report can have a learning curve and may require additional training for users who are not familiar with the system.

There could be more flexibility to have more custom fields. In our business, it all gets quite complicated, but we have to fit into a relatively rigid system. The fields you can customize are relatively constrained, and a bit more flexibility would be helpful.

The interface can feel a bit rigid sometimes, especially when you are first learning where everything lives. Setting up custom automated reconciliation rules or pulling very specific, non-standard reports can be a bit clunky and usually requires some trial and error to get the logic exactly right. It’s a powerful tool if you handle heavy reconciliation volumes, but make sure your team invests in proper training upfront to understand the data logic, otherwise, navigation can feel overwhelming at first. The initial setup was a pretty heavy lift. From an RTR perspective, the biggest challenge was the data mapping phase. Setting up the automated reconciliation rules and getting the matching logic exactly right also required a lot of trial and error. There was definitely a steep learning curve for the team initially, and it required a lot of change management and training.

Workday primarily has drawbacks with a heavy UI and vague error messaging. This often leads to troubleshooting StoreForce integration failures frequently, forcing team members into manual Excel workarounds for quick reconciliations. These system gaps disrupt flow by creating bottlenecks. Vague integration errors force the team into investigation mode, stalling regular processing. The lack of a quick math UI necessitates manual Excel efforts, doubling the work.

One downside of Workday Financial Management is that its initial implementation and configuration can be complex and time-consuming, especially for organizations with unique requirements. The reporting and customization capabilities, while powerful, often require advanced knowledge, which can create a learning curve for new users. Additionally, certain processes may feel less flexible compared to legacy systems, and troubleshooting issues can sometimes depend heavily on support or consultants.

Implementation is complicated, time consuming, and expensive (Organizations should not attempt this without an implementation partner). Post-implementation, it is difficult and painful to adapt Workday to large organizational changes or transformations. This requires FDM (foundational data model) changes which impact reporting and metrics and often require large security model changes as well. There is also a steep learning curve for IT; this often requires hiring new staff and external service providers. Reporting, although robust and powerful, also requires deep expertise or outside help. Users must switch to using Workday's terminology. For example, Workday is looking for the keyword Supplier, not Vendor. It would be nice if Workday's AI could recognize these as the similar and return results people are looking for. You can customize what results are returned for specific searches, but this requires monitoring and can be time consuming.
Some workflows feel unintuitive. Drilling down payroll details and switching between budget views can take more clicks than expected. Accessing the Workday courses can be tricky.

Rolling it out can be time-consuming and costly, especially for large organizations with legacy systems. Also, while the interface looks modern, it takes a lot of training for finance teams to feel comfortable with advanced functions. Further, customization options aren’t as deep as some older ERP platforms, so companies often have to adapt their processes to fit Workday rather than the other way around.

The 'Showcase Your Best Self' feature might need further improvement. I feel that when I provide information about myself, it's not correctly communicated to others. There should be better push communication within the organization, like when I complete a training and update my profile, it should be shared in some way.

What I dislike most is that Workday still has room to mature in its Account Reconciliation and Bank Statement Reconciliation capabilities. The native tools don’t fully support more complex reconciliation scenarios, and the processes often require manual steps or workarounds. Enhancing automation, matching rules, and workflow in these areas would bring Workday closer to being an end‑to‑end financial close solution.
Workday streamlines HR and finance processes, giving me a clear view of workflows, approvals, and employee data. It saves time and reduces errors, making daily operations smoother for our team.

One downside I’ve experienced with Workday Financial Management is that certain processes—especially the initial setup and custom reporting—can feel complex and time-consuming without proper training. At times, the system also runs slower when handling large data sets, which can reduce efficiency during peak workloads. In addition, even small configuration changes often require relying on technical teams, and that dependency can delay quick adjustments when they’re needed.

While Workday Financial Management is very powerful, some processes can feel less intuitive or require extra steps, and reporting customization can sometimes be limited, so having more flexibility and easier navigation would improve the user experience. Navigation could be improved by simplifying menus and reducing the number of clicks needed to access commonly used reports or tasks. For reporting, more drag-and-drop customization, easier filtering options, and the ability to save personalized report layouts would make it faster to generate the insights I need without relying on IT or advanced configurations. The setup of core HR and payroll data was straightforward due to clear guidance and templates, but customizing reports and configuring complex workflows presented challenges that required extra time and support.

I feel like Workday Financial Management could improve in several ways, such as simplifying navigation with clearer menus, adding customizable dashboards, and improving search functionality for faster access. The initial setup, while straightforward, also required some training to fully understand and navigate all features.

The first limitation I have observed while using Xero is that it has certain limitations regarding advanced reporting and customization options. For instance, while trying to create a report or view finances from different perspectives, one finds limited possibilities in doing so within Xero, hence having to resort to exporting the information and then manually manipulating it using other software programs. Another drawback I have experienced when working with Xero is that when reconciling transactions or dealing with several of them at once, the procedure may turn out to be rather manual and, therefore, tedious. In addition, while many third-party applications integrate well with Xero, resulting in discrepancies in information that will require manual adjustments. Finally, it should be noted that while Xero provides excellent accounting services, its pricing increases when using additional functions or involving more users, making the solution costly for large companies.

I haven’t had any major issues. There was a small learning curve at first, mainly around understanding some accounting terms and setting up categories properly, but once it was set up, it’s been straightforward.

For example, I sell a product that needs 8-10 months for fabrication, and I should be able to invoice 50 or 60% of the quote and the balance when the product is ready to ship. Also, during the initial setup, Xero announced a free migration, which was not true. We had to pay $300 as we had more than one year of data. Additionally, the migration did not bring 100% of the database, as no estimate was imported.

And then I have to authorize, but it wouldn't let me authorize that. And I seem to end up going back and forth between screens just to get this person in the system. And all I wanted to do was list it as an expense so that I could pay it.
Some advanced settings and reporting sections were a little confusing at first and took time to understand. I also noticed that new useful features seem to be available only in higher plans.

While the core platform is excellent, the tier-based feature gating can still be frustrating. For example, needing a higher-tier plan or a third-party app just for advanced inventory or multi-warehouse tracking feels like a bit of a leap for mid-sized businesses. I also think the user interface consistency needs work. While the new dashboard and reporting sections are sleek and modern, navigating back into the deeper settings or the Chart of Accounts feels like stepping back five years. A unified design language across the entire platform would make the user experience much smoother.

The 'Apple' analogy holds true here, the onboarding is beautiful, but the infrastructure requires professional attention. Xero's initial setup is a choose your own adventure experience. One downside of Xero is that some advanced features can feel overwhelming at first, especially for new users without accounting experience. While the interface is clean, there can still be a learning curve when navigating more complex reports or settings. Another limitation is pricing. As your business grows and you need additional features like payroll or multi-currency support, the subscription cost can increase significantly. Some integrations also require separate paid subscriptions. Additionally, customer support response times can sometimes be slower than expected, particularly during busy periods. While the help center is detailed, direct support isn’t always immediate.

Integration Challenges: While Xero integrates with many popular third-party applications, integrating with niche or industry-specific software can be problematic. For example, in the manufacturing industry, there are specialized inventory management systems that have unique data structures and requirements. Integrating Xero with these systems often requires significant technical expertise and may result in inconsistent data transfer. This can disrupt the seamless flow of information between different business processes, leading to inefficiencies and potential errors in financial reporting. API Limitations: For developers or businesses looking to build custom integrations, Xero's API has some restrictions. The available API endpoints may not cover all aspects of Xero's functionality. This limits the development of more comprehensive and tailored integrations. For instance, if a business wants to create a custom application that interacts with Xero's advanced accounting workflows, the lack of complete API access can be a major bottleneck.

There are a few aspects of Xero that I find less appealing, despite it being a robust platform overall. The inventory management features are quite limited—while they work for straightforward products, they fall short for businesses dealing with complex stock, assemblies, or detailed cost tracking. Customizing reports can also be a challenge; although the reporting tools are powerful once you get the hang of them, creating truly personalized reports isn’t very intuitive. Payroll functionality varies by region—while it performs well in some countries, in others it’s quite basic or requires the use of third-party applications. I’ve also noticed that pricing can escalate quickly, especially as your needs change or you start depending on paid integrations. Some manual work is still necessary, as bank rules and reconciliations aren’t as automated as I’d like. I really appreciate the find and match feature for bank reconciliation. However, it would be much more useful if it allowed reconciliation to clearing accounts as well. In my workflow, I use Jane EMR as the customer front end, which processes credit card payments and marks invoices as paid. The issue is that the funds are not deposited immediately; there is a delay of several days. To prevent problems at month end, I record these credit card payments in a clearing account. When the payments eventually arrive in the bank account as a batch, I reconcile them to the clearing account, but I have to do this manually because the find and match feature does not support it. If the intention is to keep 'Find and Match' uncluttered, perhaps there could be a separate function, such as 'Find and Match Clearing,' where you can specify an account to match from.

Any queries are recorded online and then Xero get back to the person making the query via their online portal. Generally the support is knowledgeable but due to the time taken to reply (from 2 hours to 24 hours) if you are needing an urgent reply this can be frustrating. Xero have over the years brought in a number of what they consider to be enhancements to their core bookkeeping product. These "enhancements" do not always provide a better experience for their users. Recently there was a change in their invoicing package. The changes made did not seem to be in consultation with any users and there were so many complaints that they had to delay withdrawing the old package. Many utilities of the old invoicing system which were liked by users were not carried forward and there have been hundreds of complaints on the online forums requesting some of the old functionality to be reinstated. Xero's response has been very much " we hear what you say but we are going to do nothing about it" . This does not foster good relationships with users. I know of a number of accountancy firms who are fed up and have moved or are looking to move their clients to other bookkeeping packages. There a regular price increases which do not reflect better usage or improvement to the overall software and again I know of practices who consider that Xero are pricing themselves out of the lucrative market they operate in. Overall Xero need to listen and react better to their customer base.

While the standard reports cover most use cases, building customised or more complex reports sometimes requires third-party add-ons or workarounds, which could be streamlined further. Also, the search function can feel a bit limited, especially when you're trying to locate older transactions or drill down to specific line items. A more advanced filter or search logic would make a big difference. That said, these are minor issues in the grand scheme of things. The platform’s core features reconciliation, invoicing, and bank syncing work reliably and save us a lot of time.

While Xero is a fantastic tool overall, there are a few areas where there’s room for improvement. One of the main challenges is the limited customization in reporting. While the default reports are helpful, creating more complex, tailored reports often requires workarounds or external tools. For businesses with unique reporting needs, this can be a bit limiting. Another point is the learning curve for new users. Although the platform is user-friendly once you're familiar with it, some features—like tracking categories or setting up rules for bank reconciliation—can be slightly confusing at first without proper onboarding or tutorials. Lastly, the customer support experience can sometimes feel a bit slow. Most support is via email or tickets, and in situations where you need quick, real-time assistance, it would be great to have access to live chat or phone support. That said, these are relatively minor in the grand scheme of things. The core functionalities are strong, and Xero continues to improve with updates based on user feedback—which is always encouraging.

As a user of Xero, I have found that the software has some limitations. One limitation is that it is only available as a cloud-based solution, which means that I need an internet connection in order to access it. This can be inconvenient for businesses that operate in remote locations or have unreliable internet access. Another limitation is that Xero does not offer some of the advanced features that are available in other accounting software. For example, it does not have built-in support for inventory management or job costing, which may be important for my business. In addition, Xero may not be the best option for businesses with complex financial needs. While the software is user-friendly and easy to use, it may not have the sophisticated features that I need. Finally, Xero is not free. While it offers a range of pricing plans, I will need to pay to use the service. This may not be suitable for businesses with limited budgets. Overall, while Xero has been useful for my business, it may not be suitable for everyone. Its cloud-based nature, limited advanced features, and cost may be limiting for some businesses.

While Xero is really good in many ways, there are some really basic things that it lacks, which is annoying. For example, if you create a batch payment (pay multiple bills at once), it will create a batch file, which you should be able to upload to your bank, and it will automatically create and pay the transactions. But the batch file they generate is not customizable, and therefore it does not work with many banks. Our company uses Chase Bank, which is a very large organization, and it does not meet its format requirements. Considering how many banks exist, I would think that customizing the file output would have been implemented long ago. Another example of basic functionality that is missing is bulk voiding or deleting of bill credits. For some reason, you cannot do this in bulk, which I find very odd.

Until recently, there wasn't much I didn't like. I probably couldn't name anything, apart from the somewhat dated UI - but that's a stretch. Recently however, Xero have taken a core feature (Expenses) and starting selling it as an add-on, on top of the monthly subscription fee. Expenses as you may know, and would expect, is 'included' in the monthly subscription and formed part of the core software. There was no additional charge. They've recently updated the Expenses feature (which they should be doing anyway) and now believe that because they've updated it, that warrants it being pulled from core and sold separately as an add-on. As a current subscriber, I signed up for the all inclusive package. Having features ripped from the core package and charged for in addtion to the monthly subscription is not on. Especially when no discount or reduction of the monthly subscription cost was offered. As a SaaS provider, any and all future updates, regardless of how big or small they are, should form part of the monthly subscription. Choosing an accounting package is a big decision and once you're committed, it's difficult and often costly to change. With Xero now employing this kind of model, I'd caution anyone considering Xero to think again, think hard. Today it's Expenses, tomorrow it could be something else. These disgusting tactics employed by Xero are unacceptable.
